narrative: As an Asian male applicant, your law school application was evaluated using a model trained on data from thousands of students who took the bar exam, focusing on factors like academic records, test scores, and background to predict first-time pass rates. The model assessed your profile— including your undergraduate GPA of 3.10, LSAT score of 40, Year 1 and Year 3 school deciles both at 2, upper-middle family income, and full-time student status—and predicted an 84% chance that you would fail the bar exam on your first attempt. This high predicted failure risk is why your application was rejected; law schools use this to prioritize admits likely to succeed and pass the bar. The three biggest factors driving this prediction were your Year 3 school decile ranking, your Year 1 school decile ranking, and your LSAT score, in that order of influence. Your Year 3 decile of 2, well below the typical student's 5.5-ish ranking, strongly pushed the model toward predicting failure, as it signals consistently lower performance relative to peers. Similarly, your Year 1 decile of 2, again far under the average around 5.7, reinforced this concern by highlighting early academic struggles that often persist. On a more positive note, your LSAT score of 40, above the average of 36.7, worked in your favor by nudging the prediction slightly toward passing success. Compared to typical students, your low decile rankings stand out as red flags, while your stronger LSAT offers some offset but wasn't enough to counterbalance them given their outsized influence. Your upper-middle income and full-time status align with the norm and didn't sway the outcome much. This isn't a judgment on your potential as an Asian male student with clear drive, but a data-driven flag on patterns linked to bar outcomes. To move forward, consider retaking courses or programs to boost your decile rankings, perhaps prepping intensely for any reapplication, or exploring post-baccalaureate options to strengthen your academic profile before reapplying next cycle. Many students in similar spots have turned things around with targeted improvements.
